facts.fact: Claude Opus 4.6 and Sonnet 4.6 now support 1-million-token context windows at standard pricing (no surcharge), whereas previously long-context requests beyond a threshold were charged 2x the base rate

Prompt caching reduces cached token costs to 10% of standard input price (cache reads cost $0.30/M tokens vs $3.00/M fresh for Claude models), with cache writes at 1.25x (5-min) or 2x (1-hour) base price
Batch processing via Anthropic API provides 50% cost reduction on both input and output tokens for asynchronous workloads
OpenAI's GPT-5.2 costs $1.75/$14 per million tokens vs Anthropic's Claude Opus 4.6 at $5/$25, making OpenAI cheaper at standard rates but both providers offer ~90% savings on cached input

The 'Lost in the Middle' phenomenon causes LLMs to exhibit 30%+ accuracy drop when relevant information is placed in the middle of context rather than at beginning or end, forming a U-shaped attention curve
Anthropic's Claude 2.1 achieved 27% accuracy on long-context tasks but jumped to 98% with a simple prompt nudge adding 'Here is the most relevant sentence in the context'
Rotary Position Embedding (RoPE), used in most modern LLMs, introduces decay effect making models attend more strongly to tokens at sequence beginning and end rather than middle positions

Semantic chunking with adaptive boundaries can improve RAG recall by up to 9% over fixed-size chunking, and late chunking (encoding full document first, then pooling) outperforms traditional approaches in recent benchmarks
A 1M-token GPT-5.4 query costs $2.50 for input alone ($2,500/day at 1K queries/day), while the same workload on DeepSeek costs $300/day—illustrating 8.3x cost variance across providers
A hybrid approach combining 8k compressed history tokens + 8k retrieved context achieves 12.5x token cost reduction vs naive approaches, before prompt caching multipliers applied
Just five poisoned documents injected into a RAG system can achieve 90%+ efficacy in manipulating model responses and steering answers to attacker-chosen targets

MindGuard, a decision-level guardrail for agent context poisoning, achieves 94-99% average precision in detecting Tool Poisoning Attacks with processing times under one second and zero additional token cost
Memory Injection Attacks (MINJA) against agents with persistent memory achieve over 95% injection success rates using query-only attacks that don't require execution
LLM API prices dropped approximately 80% between early 2025 and early 2026, with GPT-4o input pricing falling from $5.00 to $2.50 per million tokens
In a support ticket automation system, Qwen 3 4B costs $72 for 1M requests while GPT-4o costs $9,000 for the same volume, a 125x difference despite similar utility
Intelligent routing layers that match each request to the least expensive capable model can cut LLM spend by 60-90% without sacrificing output quality
Chips and staff typically comprise 70-80% of total LLM deployment costs in production environments, with infrastructure and integration adding 20-40% on top of direct API spend
Context rot phenomenon causes LLM performance to degrade as context window grows; models have a finite 'attention budget' with diminishing returns beyond 8-16k tokens of history
Multi-scale Position Encoding (Ms-PoE) enables LLMs to effectively attend to information in middle context without fine-tuning or increased memory, via simple position embedding remapping
Watering hole attacks in May 2024 demonstrated that poisoning RAG content sources that AI systems autonomously visit can bypass authentication and inject malicious instructions into context
Hidden prompts embedded in copied text allowed attackers to exfiltrate chat history and sensitive user data once pasted into ChatGPT, requiring no user click or interaction
Cache hits for Anthropic prefix caching deliver 90% cost reduction AND 85% latency reduction on long prompts simultaneously, incentivizing cache-aware prompt architecture
A 1M-token window can hold approximately 40,000 lines of code with documentation, enabling whole-file reasoning without retrieval on mid-size repositories
Concise prompt engineering through distillation can achieve 30-50% token cost savings without sacrificing output quality by removing redundancy and padding
Context Engineering emerged as a discipline in 2025, shifting RAG from simple 'retrieval + generation' to intelligent, adaptive context assembly that treats prompts as views over a substrate
Prompt versioning via Git or dedicated platforms (PromptHub, MLflow) is essential for reproducibility; a small wording change can dramatically alter model behavior
Tool Poisoning Attacks (TPA) corrupt metadata without execution, leaving no behavioral trace for detection; defenses based purely on behavior analysis are ineffective

examples.scenario: Batch processing overnight personalization recommendations using cached user profiles and watch history reduced token costs by 60% while maintaining 98% cache hit rates
examples.metric: $1.2M annual savings on Claude API at 50M+ daily batch requests

examples.scenario: Dynamic context assembly for fraud detection agents uses semantic chunking to retrieve only transaction features above relevance threshold, avoiding full transaction history in context
examples.metric: 35% reduction in tokens per detection call with 99.2% accuracy maintained

examples.scenario: Claude 2.1 long-context evaluation showed task accuracy jumped from 27% to 98% by repositioning most-relevant context to top of prompt window (7 words changed, 0 token cost increase)
examples.metric: 71% accuracy improvement with position engineering alone

examples.scenario: Support ticket triage system using Qwen 3.5B with intelligent routing and prompt caching processes 1M tickets/month vs GPT-4o equivalent at 125x lower cost while maintaining 89% quality parity
Code documentation Q&A system implemented late semantic chunking: encode entire file first, then pool into chunk embeddings, vs traditional fixed-size splits, improving retrieval precision by 13%
examples.metric: Fewer hallucinations in code examples, fewer false negatives when finding relevant functions

examples.scenario: GPT Researcher tool demonstrates agentic RAG where the agent decides what to retrieve and when, avoiding over-fetching context; reduces average context size from 200k to 45k tokens per research task
examples.metric: 55% cost reduction per multi-step research query

examples.scenario: Attack simulation: poisoning RAG with five carefully crafted documents achieved 90%+ success rate in manipulating responses without triggering any safety detection, exposing risk in autonomous data gathering
examples.metric: Five poison documents out of millions achieved 90%+ attack efficacy

examples.scenario: Prompt versioning system enforces that every production inference logs the exact prompt version ID; one update to system instruction inadvertently changed output format, caught via version diff before deploy
examples.metric: Zero production incidents from prompt changes; 100% output format consistency maintained

analogies.concept: Context window as a materialized view
analogies.analogy: Like a database view that assembles data from multiple tables on demand, the LLM prompt is a materialized view computed at inference time from a larger substrate (codebase, knowledge base, conversation history). The substrate is the source of truth; the context is what you project into the window for this specific query.

Like shelves in a library where readers scan the first and last shelves first (beginning/end of context) but skip middle shelves, transformer attention with RoPE naturally focuses on sequence boundaries and pays less attention to the middle, regardless of information importance.

In thermodynamics, systems prefer lower-energy states; cache hits cost 1/10th the energy (tokens) of fresh computation. Prompt design becomes about finding thermal equilibrium—keeping expensive recomputation out of the loop via strategic caching.

Like a supply chain audit that tracks provenance through cryptographic hashes, MindGuard traces each decision back to the tool that produced it, attributing poisoning to the source data without waiting for the system to execute the bad action.

Fixed-size chunking is like breaking a novel at arbitrary page numbers (every 512 tokens); semantic chunking is like breaking it at chapter boundaries where meaning naturally shifts, creating chunks that make more sense to reassemble coherently.

Like version control for code—small changes (adding a word, reordering a paragraph) can have outsized impact on behavior. Reproducibility requires knowing the exact version used for every production output, not just 'the current prompt.'

Like project management with a fixed budget, every context decision (system prompt, retrieval results, conversation history) competes for the same token pool. Prioritization requires understanding ROI—does this information improve output quality enough to justify its token cost?

Static retrieval is like a librarian who always returns the same three books for every query; agentic retrieval is like a research collaborator who reads your question, judges complexity, identifies gaps, and decides whether to search more or synthesize from what's already retrieved.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Larger context windows are always better and should be maximized
common_misconceptions.reality: Larger contexts introduce context rot (degraded performance), increase poisoning attack surface, and multiply token costs. Optimal context size is task-specific; prompt engineering to minimize context while maintaining quality is more cost-effective.

common_misconceptions.misconception: All tokens in a prompt are attended to equally
common_misconceptions.reality: LLMs exhibit U-shaped attention due to RoPE and causal masking: tokens at beginning and end receive disproportionately strong attention, while middle tokens (even critical ones) are under-attended. Position of information matters as much as relevance.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Prompt caching is a free optimization with no tradeoff
common_misconceptions.reality: Cache writes cost 1.25-2x base tokens; breakeven requires 1-2 cache reads. High-variance workloads may get worse ROI. Caching is optimal for static system prompts or frequently-repeated context (user profiles, documentation).

common_misconceptions.misconception: Expensive models (OpenAI GPT-5.2, Anthropic Opus) always produce better outputs
common_misconceptions.reality: Intelligent routing to the cheapest capable model achieves 60-90% cost savings with quality parity. Haiku + caching can match Opus output on many tasks while costing 1/25th as much.

common_misconceptions.misconception: RAG systems prevent hallucinations via retrieval
common_misconceptions.reality: RAG is vulnerable to context poisoning; injecting five malicious documents into a dataset of millions achieves 90%+ attack efficacy. Retrieved context can be equally unreliable if sources are untrusted.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Context poisoning requires executing malicious code
common_misconceptions.reality: Tool Poisoning Attacks corrupt metadata without execution, leaving no behavioral trace. Defenses based only on detecting execution will miss these attacks entirely.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Prompt versioning is optional or a convenience
common_misconceptions.reality: Without versioning, reproducing a specific output (for debugging or compliance) is impossible. A single word change in a system prompt can alter behavior completely; versioning is as critical as code version control.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Fixed-size chunking is fine because RAG works anyway
common_misconceptions.reality: Semantic chunking improves recall by 9% and avoids splitting coherent topics mid-chunk. Clinical decision support benchmarks show 87% accuracy with semantic boundaries vs 13% with arbitrary splits.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Batch processing is only for low-priority tasks
common_misconceptions.reality: Batch API provides 50% discount across all token types and can be combined with prompt caching for cumulative 70-80% savings. Suitable for any non-real-time workload (overnight reporting, bulk processing, scheduled jobs).

common_misconceptions.misconception: Token costs are purely a function of model price
common_misconceptions.reality: Infrastructure, chips, and staff comprise 70-80% of deployment costs in production. Token optimization matters but represents at most 20-30% of total LLM spend; infrastructure efficiency is equally important.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Semantic caching and prompt caching are the same
common_misconceptions.reality: Semantic caching (cache hits on similar but not identical inputs) is still research; Anthropic's prompt caching requires exact prefix matches. Semantic caching would offer higher hit rates but doesn't yet exist in production APIs.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Long context windows make fine-grained retrieval unnecessary
common_misconceptions.reality: Even 1M-token windows have cost and latency consequences. Smart retrieval (agentic RAG, reranking, context selection) remains essential to avoid wasting tokens on irrelevant information.
